section.title: Woe to the Obstinate Nation
section.sectionRationale: This long woe, within the sequence of woes confronting false trust, chapters 28 to 33, confronts Judah's rebellious alliance with Egypt formed without asking the LORD. It moves from rejected prophecy and refused rest, through the LORD's longing to be gracious and the promise that His people's Teacher will no longer hide Himself, to a final vision of the LORD's fury breaking Assyria at Topheth.
section.thesis: Judah's rebellious flight to Egypt for help, formed without asking the LORD and against His will, exposes a people who reject true prophecy and refuse repentance and rest, yet the LORD still longs to be gracious, promising that their Teacher will no longer hide Himself and that He Himself will finally shatter Assyria who threatens them.
section.frameNote: Six movements: woe to those who flee to Egypt without asking the LORD; the rebellious children who reject true prophecy; in repentance and rest you would be saved, but you were not willing; the LORD longs to be gracious, your Teacher will no longer hide; rain and healing on the day the LORD binds up His people; and the LORD's fury shattering Assyria at Topheth.

units.label: Woe to those who flee to Egypt
units.note: an alliance formed without asking the LORD

verses.text: “Woe to the rebellious children,” declares the LORD, “to those who carry out a plan that is not Mine, who form an alliance, but against My will, heaping up sin upon sin.
They set out to go down to Egypt without asking My advice, to seek shelter under Pharaoh's protection and take refuge in Egypt's shade.
But Pharaoh's protection will become your shame, and the refuge of Egypt's shade your disgrace.
For though their princes are at Zoan and their envoys have arrived in Hanes,
everyone will be put to shame because of a people useless to them. They cannot be of help; they are good for nothing but shame and reproach.

This is the burden against the beasts of the Negev: Through a land of hardship and distress, of lioness and lion, of viper and flying serpent, they carry their wealth on the backs of donkeys and their treasures on the humps of camels, to a people of no profit to them.
Egypt's help is futile and empty; therefore I have called her Rahab Who Sits Still.
Go now, write it on a tablet in their presence and inscribe it on a scroll; it will be for the days to come, a witness forever and ever.
These are rebellious people, deceitful children, children unwilling to obey the LORD's instruction.
They say to the seers, “Stop seeing visions!” and to the prophets, “Do not prophesy to us the truth! Speak to us pleasant words; prophesy illusions.
Get out of the way; turn off the road. Rid us of the Holy One of Israel!”
Therefore this is what the Holy One of Israel says: “Because you have rejected this message, trusting in oppression and relying on deceit,
this iniquity of yours is like a breach about to fail, a bulge in a high wall, whose collapse will come suddenly, in an instant!

It will break in pieces like a potter's jar, shattered so that no fragment can be found. Not a shard will be found in the dust large enough to scoop the coals from a hearth or to skim the water from a cistern.

units.label: In repentance and rest you would be saved

verses.text: For the Lord GOD, the Holy One of Israel, has said: “By {{shuvah|repentance}} and rest you would be saved; your strength would lie in quiet confidence, but you were not willing.”
“No,” you say, “we will flee on horses.” Therefore you will flee! “We will ride swift horses,” but your pursuers will be faster.
A thousand will flee at the threat of one; at the threat of five you will all flee, until you are left alone like a pole on a mountaintop, like a banner on a hill.

units.label: The LORD longs to be gracious
units.note: your Teacher will no longer hide

verses.text: Therefore the LORD longs to be gracious to you; therefore He rises to show you compassion, for the LORD is a just God. Blessed are all who wait for Him.
O people in Zion who dwell in Jerusalem, you will weep no more. He will surely be gracious when you cry for help; when He hears, He will answer you.
The Lord will give you the bread of adversity and the water of affliction, but your {{moreh|Teacher}} will no longer hide Himself, with your own eyes you will see Him.
And whether you turn to the right or to the left, your ears will hear this command behind you: “This is the way. Walk in it.”
So you will desecrate your silver-plated idols and your gold-plated images. You will throw them away like menstrual cloths, saying to them, “Be gone!”

units.label: Rain and healing on the day the LORD binds up His people

verses.text: Then He will send rain for the seed that you have sown in the ground, and the food that comes from your land will be rich and plentiful. On that day your cattle will graze in open pastures.
The oxen and donkeys that work the ground will eat salted fodder, winnowed with shovel and pitchfork.
And from every high mountain and every raised hill, streams of water will flow in the day of great slaughter, when the towers fall.

The light of the moon will be as bright as the sun, and the light of the sun will be seven times brighter, like the light of seven days, on the day that the LORD binds up the brokenness of His people and heals the wounds He has inflicted.
Behold, the Name of the LORD comes from afar, with burning anger and dense smoke. His lips are full of fury, and His tongue is like a consuming fire.
His breath is like a rushing torrent that rises to the neck. He comes to sift the nations in a sieve of destruction; He bridles the jaws of the peoples to lead them astray.
You will sing as on the night of a holy festival, and your heart will rejoice like one who walks to the music of a flute, going up to the mountain of the LORD, to the Rock of Israel.
And the LORD will cause His majestic voice to be heard and His mighty arm to be revealed, striking in angry wrath with a flame of consuming fire, and with cloudburst, storm, and hailstones.
For Assyria will be shattered at the voice of the LORD; He will strike them with His scepter.
And with every stroke of the rod of punishment that the LORD brings down on them, the tambourines and lyres will sound as He battles with weapons brandished.

For Topheth has long been prepared; it has been made ready for the king. Its funeral pyre is deep and wide, with plenty of fire and wood. The breath of the LORD, like a torrent of burning sulfur, sets it ablaze.

shuvah.gloss: Repentance, returning, or turning back. The LORD's offer, by repentance and rest you would be saved, ties genuine turning back to Him with the quiet confidence that trusts rather than schemes, a path the people refused in favor of frantic alliance-making.

moreh.gloss: Teacher, one who instructs authoritatively. The promise that your Teacher will no longer hide Himself, but will be seen with your own eyes, anticipates a personal, visible instruction from God far beyond the words of any human prophet, ultimately fulfilled when the Word became flesh and was seen, John 1:14.

basic.context: Judah's leaders, facing Assyrian pressure, secretly sent envoys to Egypt seeking a military alliance, without ever consulting the LORD. Isaiah exposes this as rebellion, since the people also silenced true prophets who spoke hard truths, preferring pleasant illusions instead. Yet even this obstinate nation receives the LORD's longing offer of grace, the promise that their Teacher will one day be seen with their own eyes, and the assurance that Assyria itself will finally be shattered by the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes a real historical crisis, likely during Hezekiah's reign around 705 to 701 BC, when Judah's officials pursued an Egyptian alliance against Assyria against Isaiah's explicit counsel. The chapter's climax, verses 18 to 26, reaches beyond that immediate crisis toward a fuller future hope, the Teacher seen with one's own eyes and idols discarded, language that finds its deepest fulfillment in the incarnation and gospel rather than any single ancient event.
basic.originalLanguages: The LORD offers shuvah, repentance and returning, paired with quiet confidence, as the true path of salvation, which the people refused in favor of frantic scheming. He promises that their moreh, Teacher, will no longer hide Himself but will be seen with their own eyes, language the New Testament fulfills when the eternal Word becomes visible and touchable in Jesus Christ, John 1:14, 1 John 1:1.
basic.typology: The Teacher who will no longer hide Himself but will be seen with one's own eyes anticipates the incarnation of Christ, God's own instruction made visible and personal. The command heard from behind, this is the way, walk in it, anticipates Christ's own declaration, I am the way, John 14:6.
basic.christ: The promise that Judah's Teacher will no longer hide Himself, but will be seen with their own eyes, finds its fulfillment when the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we saw His glory, John 1:14, and when the apostles could say, we have seen with our eyes, concerning Him, 1 John 1:1. The voice behind them saying, this is the way, walk in it, anticipates Jesus' own words, I am the way, the truth, and the life, John 14:6.
basic.god: God is grieved by His people's rebellion, their scheming apart from Him and their preference for pleasant lies over hard truth, yet He still longs to be gracious and rises to show compassion, since He is a just God who blesses all who wait for Him. His purposes for Judah move through discipline toward a promised, personal, visible instruction and toward the final shattering of the very power, Assyria, that Judah feared enough to seek Egypt's help against.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 18:19-24 records the historical Egyptian alliance and Assyrian mockery of it during this crisis. John 1:14 declares the Word became flesh, fulfilling the promise of a Teacher no longer hidden. John 14:6 has Jesus declaring Himself the way, echoing the voice behind them in verse 21. 2 Kings 19:35-37 records Assyria's own eventual, sudden destruction, a partial historical fulfillment of the LORD shattering Assyria described here.
basic.oneStory: This chapter exposes a rebellious people who scheme apart from God and silence hard truths, yet meets that rebellion with the LORD's longing grace, the promise of a Teacher who will no longer hide, and the certainty that even Assyria will be shattered. It anticipates the whole gospel pattern: God's patient grace toward a stubborn people, culminating in the Teacher made visible in Christ, the way in whom we are to walk.

context.literary: A long, layered woe oracle moving from historical rebuke, the Egyptian alliance, through rejected prophecy and refused rest, to an extended promise of grace and instruction, and closing with an extended, almost liturgical description of the LORD's fiery judgment on Assyria, giving the chapter both an intimate, pastoral tone and a cosmic, warlike one.
context.historical: Set most plausibly during Hezekiah's reign, when Judah's officials, against Isaiah's counsel, sent envoys to Egypt seeking military help against Assyrian pressure around 705 to 701 BC; the eventual, sudden destruction of Sennacherib's army, 2 Kings 19:35-37, provides a real historical anchor for this chapter's climactic vision of Assyria shattered by the LORD's own voice.
context.canonical: Continues the sequence of woes, chapters 28 to 33, confronting misplaced trust, particularly in Egypt, a theme picked up again in chapter 31; its language of a Teacher seen with one's own eyes anticipates the incarnation motifs developed fully in John's Gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose Judah's rebellious scheming and rejection of true prophecy as sin against the LORD, while assuring His people that His grace still waits for them, and that repentance and quiet trust, not frantic alliance, is the true path to salvation and to seeing their Teacher revealed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter describes a specific historical crisis, misplaced Egyptian alliance and rejected prophecy; it is not prescribing a specific foreign policy for every era. Its abiding principle is that repentance and quiet trust in God, not anxious self-reliant scheming, is always the true path to security, a principle as relevant today as in Hezekiah's time.

debates.question: How much of the Teacher promise, verse 20, God's people finally seeing Him with their own eyes, was understood by Isaiah's first hearers, and how much awaits the incarnation?

views.label: A near hope of restored access to faithful prophetic teaching
views.case: Some read this as promising that after the coming discipline, God's people would again have clear access to faithful prophets and teachers, no longer having true instruction obscured or hidden as in the sealed scroll of chapter 29.

views.label: A promise reaching toward the incarnation itself
views.case: The specific language, seeing the Teacher with your own eyes, exceeds ordinary prophetic access and anticipates something more direct and personal, fulfilled precisely when the Word became flesh and was seen and touched by human eyes and hands, John 1:14, 1 John 1:1.

debates.bearing: As with other passages in this section of Isaiah, a near and far fulfillment can coexist: the immediate hope of restored, faithful teaching after judgment finds its ultimate and far richer fulfillment in the incarnate Christ, God's own instruction made visible in a person rather than words alone.

study.originalLanguages: The LORD's offer of shuvah, repentance and turning back, paired with quiet confidence, describes the trust Judah refused in favor of frantic scheming with Egypt. His promise that their moreh, Teacher, will no longer hide Himself but be seen with their own eyes uses language of direct, personal revelation that the New Testament shows fulfilled when the eternal Word became visible flesh, John 1:14.

typology.type: The Teacher who will no longer hide Himself but will be seen with your own eyes
typology.fulfillment: The incarnation of Christ, the Word made flesh, seen and touched, John 1:14, 1 John 1:1.

typology.type: The voice behind you saying, this is the way, walk in it
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own declaration, I am the way, the truth, and the life, John 14:6.

study.christ: This chapter's promise that Judah's Teacher will no longer hide Himself, but will be seen with their own eyes, is fulfilled in the incarnation, when the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen His glory, John 1:14, and the apostle John could testify, that which we have seen with our eyes, concerning Him, 1 John 1:1. The voice heard behind them, saying this is the way, walk in it, anticipates Jesus' own words to His disciples, I am the way, the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through Me, John 14:6, the true and living path Judah's frantic scheming with Egypt could never provide.
study.god: God is grieved by rebellious scheming and the rejection of hard prophetic truth in favor of pleasant illusions, yet His grief does not overwhelm His grace; He longs to be gracious and rises to show compassion to a people who did not deserve it. His justice ensures that Assyria, the very power Judah feared, will itself be shattered, while His mercy ensures that His own people will finally see their Teacher revealed and hear His voice clearly directing their way.

crossReferences.note: Records the Assyrian field commander's mockery of Judah's misplaced trust in Egyptian help, the historical backdrop for this chapter's rebuke.
The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, fulfilling the promise that Judah's Teacher would no longer hide Himself but be seen.
Jesus declares Himself the way, fulfilling the voice heard behind Judah saying, this is the way, walk in it.
Records the sudden, historical destruction of Sennacherib's Assyrian army, a partial fulfillment of this chapter's vision of Assyria shattered by the LORD.

study.oneStory: This chapter exposes Judah's rebellious scheming with Egypt and rejection of true prophecy, yet meets it with the LORD's longing grace, the promise of a Teacher who will no longer hide, and the certain shattering of Assyria's threat. It anticipates the whole gospel pattern of God's patient grace toward a stubborn people, fulfilled when the Teacher was made visible in Christ, the way in whom His people are called to walk.

sermon.title: Battling the Unbelief of Impatience
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly on Isaiah 30:1-5, addressing Judah's impatient, unbelieving rush to Egypt for help instead of waiting on the LORD, the very sin this chapter exposes and the quiet confidence it calls God's people to instead.

quiz.q: What sin does the LORD specifically name in verses 1 to 2 regarding the alliance with Egypt?

options.text: Forming an alliance that was not His plan and going to Egypt without asking His advice.
options.note: Verses 1 to 2 name exactly this: a plan not from the LORD, formed without consulting Him.

options.text: Refusing to form any alliances with foreign nations at all.
options.note: The opposite; the sin named is forming an alliance without consulting God, not refusing alliances.

options.text: Worshiping Egyptian gods exclusively.
options.note: Not the specific charge here; the focus is on political alliance without seeking God's counsel.

options.text: Failing to pay proper tribute to Assyria.
options.note: The chapter's concern is misplaced trust in Egypt, not tribute obligations to Assyria.

quiz.q: What do the people ask the prophets to do instead of speaking truth, verses 10 to 11?

options.text: Speak pleasant words and prophesy illusions instead of the truth.
options.note: Verse 10 states this request directly and explicitly.

options.text: Speak only in written form, never verbally.
options.note: Not the request made; the issue is content, truth versus pleasant illusions, not the medium of speech.

options.text: Prophesy only about foreign nations, never about Judah.
options.note: Not the specific request in this text; they want pleasant illusions generally, not merely a change of subject.

options.text: Increase the frequency of prophetic messages.
options.note: The opposite; they want the prophets to stop seeing visions altogether and speak comforting lies instead.

quiz.q: What does verse 15 say would have brought salvation and strength?

options.text: Repentance and rest, quiet confidence in the LORD.
options.note: Verse 15 states this precisely, though the people were not willing to embrace it.

options.text: A larger and better equipped army.
options.note: The opposite point; the chapter critiques reliance on military strength and swift horses instead of trust in God.

options.text: A stronger political alliance with Assyria itself.
options.note: Not what verse 15 recommends; it commends repentance and quiet trust in the LORD, not another alliance.

options.text: Building higher city walls.
options.note: Not the specific means of salvation named in this verse, which is repentance and quiet confidence in God.

quiz.q: What promise is given about the people's Teacher in verse 20?

options.text: Their Teacher will no longer hide Himself; they will see Him with their own eyes.
options.note: Verse 20 gives this striking promise of direct, visible revelation.

options.text: Their Teacher will remain permanently hidden from them.
options.note: The opposite; the promise is that the Teacher will no longer be hidden.

options.text: They will need to travel to Egypt to find their Teacher.
options.note: Not stated in this verse; the promise concerns the Teacher being seen directly, not a location to travel to.

options.text: Only the priests will be permitted to see their Teacher.
options.note: The promise is addressed broadly to God's people, not restricted to priests alone.

quiz.q: What ultimately happens to Assyria according to the chapter's final section, verses 27 to 33?

options.text: Assyria will be shattered at the voice of the LORD, struck with His scepter.
options.note: Verse 31 states this directly: Assyria's shattering comes from the LORD's own voice and power.

options.text: Assyria will conquer and absorb Judah permanently.
options.note: The opposite; the passage describes Assyria's own destruction, not Judah's absorption.

options.text: Assyria and Egypt will form a lasting alliance against Judah.
options.note: Not described in this passage; the focus is on Assyria's shattering by the LORD, not a new alliance.

options.text: Assyria will peacefully withdraw without any conflict.
options.note: The imagery is intensely violent, fire, storm, and hailstones, not a peaceful withdrawal.

questions: Judah formed an alliance without ever asking God's advice. Where in your own decision-making do you tend to act first and pray or seek God's counsel only afterward, if at all?
The people asked prophets to speak pleasant illusions rather than hard truth. Are there areas in your life where you would rather hear comforting lies than the truth that could actually help you?

God says that repentance and quiet confidence would have brought salvation, but the people were not willing. What would it look like for you to choose quiet trust over frantic self-reliant scheming this week?

The LORD longs to be gracious despite His people's rebellion. How does knowing that God actively longs to show you compassion, rather than reluctantly tolerating you, change how you approach Him after failure?

The promise is that God's people will finally see their Teacher with their own eyes, fulfilled in Christ. How present and visible does Christ feel to you right now, and what might help you see Him more clearly through His word?

translationNotes.note: Rahab Who Sits Still is a sarcastic renaming of Egypt, using a term elsewhere applied to a chaos monster or to Egypt itself, here mocking Egypt's inability to act despite her reputation for strength.
Where BSB prints an em dash before with your own eyes you will see Him, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
Where BSB prints em dashes around like the light of seven days, they have been rendered as commas per house style.
Topheth was a site associated with pagan child sacrifice in the Valley of Hinnom; here it becomes, ironically, the place prepared for the destruction of the Assyrian king himself.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 30:1-33. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
